pull-from-notion.prompt:
Read all Candidate rows from the AI Brief Story Pool for the current week. The database is 'AI Brief Story Pool' in the connected Notion workspace. Filter by Status=Candidate AND Week=[current ISO week]. Pull the full row data for each: Story Title, Announcement Summary, What It Means, Analogy, Source, Source Link, Source Date, Capture Source, Lens, Relevance, Resonance, and any existing content in other fields. Group results by client (Relevance multi-select values). Also read the following client profiles and hard rules that must be applied during selection and tailoring.

CLIENT PROFILES AND SENSITIVITIES:

**Incserv** — Business: Corporate registered-agent and compliance-filing service. Reader: CEO/owner. Lens priority: (1) Risk & Regulatory, (2) Cyber, (3) Vendor & Platform, (4) Workforce & Operating Model, (5) Capital & Deals. What lands: state-level AI legislation, regulatory/reporting requirements, corporate transparency, legal AI tooling, cybersecurity regulation, entity management compliance. Translation lens: 'what this means for the registered agent business, corporate compliance, and the operational stack of a filings company.'

**M&T Bank** — Business: Regional bank (~$200B assets) operating in the northeast/mid-Atlantic. Reader: regional bank president. Lens priority: (1) Risk & Regulatory, (2) Cyber, (3) Vendor & Platform, (4) Workforce & Operating Model, (5) Capital & Deals. What lands: OCC/FDIC/Fed AI actions, AML/KYC compliance, call center AI, lending/underwriting AI, community bank compliance. What does not land: pure investment banking or capital-markets items. Translation lens: 'what this means for a regional bank with both a compliance and a growth lens.'

**Brown Advisory** — Business: Independent investment management and strategic advisory firm. Reader: CIO or senior investment strategist. Lens priority: (1) Capital & Deals, (2) Cyber, (3) Risk & Regulatory, (4) Workforce & Operating Model. What lands: AI company valuations, M&A involving AI targets, AI-driven investment platforms, wealth-management AI, SEC actions on AI tools in advisory. Translation lens: 'what this means for an investment manager allocating capital and advising wealthy families and institutions.'

**Alpine Associates** — Business: Activist hedge fund. Reader: managing partner or senior analyst. Lens priority: (1) Capital & Deals, (2) Risk & Regulatory, (3) Cyber. What lands: AI-driven M&A, activist opportunities tied to AI adoption, AI valuation disconnects, regulatory shifts that create mispricing. Translation lens: 'what this means for an activist investor looking for dislocation, forced change, and AI-as-catalyst plays.'

**KPS Life** — Business: Functional Service Provider (FSP) for clinical trials, headquartered in Malvern PA. Embeds clinical professionals (CRAs, monitors, scientists, regulatory) inside pharma and biotech sponsor environments. Reader: firm leader who sees the world through what sponsors need and what embedded teams deliver. Lens priority: (1) Clinical & Trials, (2) Risk & Regulatory, (3) Workforce & Operating Model, (4) Cyber. HARD SENSITIVITY: KPS sells embedded people. Never frame AI-and-labor stories as headcount obsolescence. Always frame as leverage for embedded experts. If an item cannot be framed that way for KPS, drop it from KPS's brief. Correct: 'AI in trial monitoring makes embedded monitors more effective by handling data volume.' NEVER: 'AI replaces clinical monitors in trials.' What lands: FDA RFI deadlines, real-time clinical trial pilots, FDA infrastructure announcements, sponsor-science direction items, state-level healthcare AI regulation, anything that helps KPS position embedded clinical expertise as essential. What does not land: pure capital-markets items, consumer AI, workforce-replacement framings (forbidden). Translation lens: 'what this means for sponsor environments, and for how embedded clinical expertise stays at the center of trustworthy AI in trials.'

**ChristianaCare** — Business: Major mid-Atlantic health system. Reader: sits on the Quality & Safety Committee. Lens priority: (1) Clinical & Trials, (2) Risk & Regulatory, (3) Cyber, (4) Workforce & Operating Model. HARD SENSITIVITY: Always frame through patient safety and clinical accountability. Never frame AI adoption stories as cost-cutting or headcount reduction. Frame as quality improvement, safety enhancement, or clinical effectiveness. What lands: FDA actions (Elsa, HALO, RFI updates, clearances, real-time trial pilots), ambient AI clinical deployments, state-level AI healthcare regulation, biomedical foundation-model research, patient-safety governance items. What does not land: capital-markets-only items, consumer AI without health implications. Translation lens: 'what this means for safe, effective AI adoption inside a health system, with patient safety and clinical accountability as the organizing principles.'

**Luna Diabetes** — Business: Early-stage medtech building closed-loop insulin delivery for insulin pen users. Pre-FDA submission. Reader: founder or senior leader, strategy-and-engineering audience, capital-aware. Two-lens model: (1) Risk and capital: FDA, device cybersecurity, capital signals, regulatory environment for AI medical devices; (2) Engineering velocity: agentic coding, engineering tooling, AI in development workflows. SENSITIVITY: Do not include specific headcount or financial figures for Luna. Frame as 'lean team scaling toward commercialization' or 'small engineering team.' Do not assert revenue, runway, or employee counts. What lands: FDA actions on AI medical devices, device cybersecurity guidance, AI capability as priced investor asset framing, agentic coding tooling, Anthropic engineering practices applied to device-AI containment. What does not land: capital-markets-only items without medtech implications, hospital-system operational stories, pure research without device-AI relevance. Translation lens: 'what this means for Luna as a lean, pre-commercial medtech building a safe and credible connected device.'

select-and-tailor.prompt:
You are the Friday editor for the Keel3 'Heads Down to Heads Up' weekly AI intelligence brief. Build this week's briefs for all 7 clients.

INPUT: The staged Notion pull containing all Candidate rows for the current week, grouped by client relevance, plus full client profiles and hard rules (loaded in the previous step and staged in workspace/run/brief-assembly).

SELECTION RULES:
1. Pick 4-5 stories per client from the Candidate pool.
2. Lead with a universal headline if one exists this week, framed per client.
3. Prefer concrete, in-the-tape developments (specific regulatory actions, deals, deployments) over abstract commentary.
4. Deliberately mix Lens categories so no brief reads narrow.
5. Match items to the actual reader's role and decision lens (not just the client's industry in the abstract).
6. Drop research-direction items when operational items compete for the same slot.
7. Hard 2-sentence cap on the heavy fields to keep the one-page brief readable.

HARD CLIENT SENSITIVITIES — NEVER VIOLATE:
- KPS Life: Never frame AI as headcount replacement. Always frame as leverage for embedded experts.
- ChristianaCare: Always frame through patient safety and clinical accountability. Never cost-cutting.
- Luna Diabetes: Never assert specific revenue, headcount, runway, or employee counts. 'Lean team scaling toward commercialization.'
- M&T Bank: No pure investment-banking or capital-markets-only items.
- Alpine Associates: Frame through activist lens — dislocation, forced change, mispricing.

TAILORING WORKFLOW (per client, per story):
1. Rewrite the 'What It Means' for THIS client's industry/role/lens.
2. Write the 'What It Means For [Client]' column — 3-5 sentences tying the story to the client's operational reality, ending with a concrete recommended action.
3. Write a 'THE WEEK IN ONE LINE' synthesis sentence tying all of that client's stories together.
4. Write a one-line rationale for 'If Used, Why?' for each story selected — why this story made the cut for this client (1 sentence).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
For each of the 7 clients, produce:
- Client name
- 4-5 selected stories, each with: Story Title, Announcement Summary (keep the shared layer), What It Means (rewritten for this client), What It Means For [Client] (bespoke, 3-5 sentences + action), Analogy (keep shared if it still fits, adjust if needed), If Used Why (new — 1 sentence rationale)
- Week In One Line (one sentence, tying the stories together)

The finished output for each client should be structured as a complete brief ready for PDF rendering. Stage the results as JSON arrays in workspace/run/brief-assembly, one file per client.

render-pdfs.prompt:
Render 7 branded one-page PDF briefs from the tailored client content staged in workspace/run/brief-assembly.

For each client, produce a single-page Letter-portrait PDF using this exact brand-locked format:

BRAND SPECS:
- Navy #0F1E2E — header/footer bands, story section headings
- Orange #F25C2A — accent rules, numbered item circles, category-label pill
- Cream #F0EFE9 — analogy background box
- Slate #3A4A5C — secondary text, source attribution
- Inter font — throughout
- White body background

LAYOUT (top to bottom):
1. HEADER BAND (Navy background): Keel3 logo/wordmark left, centered title 'Happenings in AI Last Week', 'WEEKLY INTELLIGENCE BRIEF' overline, 'PREPARED FOR [Client Name]' top-right, 'WEEK OF [date range]' below
2. COLUMN HEADERS: ANNOUNCEMENTS | WHAT IT MEANS | WHAT IT MEANS FOR: [CLIENT]
3. Exactly 3-5 ITEM ROWS, each three columns:
   - LEFT (Announcements): numbered circle (Orange), bold headline, italic source + date, 2-3 sentence factual recap. Source hyperlinked.
   - MIDDLE (What It Means): 2-3 sentence universal interpretation. Cream-background analogy box: 'Like...' one sentence.
   - RIGHT (What It Means For Client): Orange category-label pill at top (derived from client's business — e.g. 'FILING THROUGHPUT', 'PATIENT SAFETY GOVERNANCE', 'EMBEDDED EXPERT LEVERAGE'), 3-5 sentences tying to the client's operational reality, ending with a concrete recommended action.
4. FOOTER BAND (Navy background): 'THE WEEK IN ONE LINE' — one sentence synthesis. Source attribution line, Keel3 URL.
5. Disclaimer: 'This brief is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal, regulatory, or financial advice.'

CONSTRAINTS:
- ONE PAGE ONLY — content must fit a single Letter portrait page.
- No em dashes anywhere. Use colons, commas, or reword.
- No hype vocabulary (disrupt, synergy, game-changer, seamless).
- Confident, neutral, professional tone — written for a busy executive.
- Every specific number must be sourced and verified.

OUTPUT: Save each PDF as Keel3_AI_Brief_[ClientName]_[Week]_2026.pdf to workspace/run/brief-assembly.

save-and-mark.prompt:
Two-part final step:

PART 1 — Upload to Google Drive: Upload all 7 rendered PDF briefs from workspace/run/brief-assembly to Google Drive. Place them in a folder called 'Keel3 Briefs' (create it if it doesn't exist). Use the naming convention: Keel3_AI_Brief_[ClientName]_[Week]_2026.pdf.

PART 2 — Mark rows as Used in Notion: For every story that was selected and used in any client's brief this week, update its row in the AI Brief Story Pool database (connected as notion:33bf7118-384a-8256-a86c-01c08c40b991). Set Status = 'Used' (select option). Also populate the 'If Used, Why?' field (rich_text) with the rationale produced during the select-and-tailor step. Only update rows that were selected — leave unselected Candidate rows as-is.
